---
title: "Lab 7"
format: docx
editor: visual
author: "Evan O"
---

## 1. Sometimes we can avoid for-loops.

Doing computations in different ways.

```{r}

# Show your result for each part. They should be the same.

# (a) for loop

#n <- 10
n <- 20
x <- 0
for (num in 1:n){
  x <- x + num**3
}
x

# (b) without loops

(sum((1:n)**3))
# (c) formula (require Math knowledge)



```

## 2. Bootstrapped Sampling with for-loops

```{r}

library(ggplot2)


# This code will generate a vector of 500 data points. Let us assume this is a 
# representative sample of the population.

x1 <- runif(500, 0, 10)

#(a) histogram of the sample. Adjust the binwidth value so
# your histogram looks informative.
df <- as.data.frame(x1)
head(df)
ggplot(df, aes(x = x1)) + geom_histogram(fill = "green", col="blue")

quantile(x1, 0.1)

#(b) 

x2 <- sample(x1, size = 500, replace = TRUE)
quantile(x2, 0.1)
#yes they are quite similar

#(c)
q10 <- c()
for (num in 1:3000){
  x2 <- sample(x1, size = 500, replace = TRUE)
  new <- as.vector(quantile(x2, 0.1))
  q10 <- c(q10, new)
  
}
#print(q10)

#(d) histogram of approximate sampling distribution. Adjust the binwidth value so
# your histogram looks informative.
q10 <- as.data.frame(q10)
ggplot(q10, aes(x=q10)) + geom_histogram(fill = "green", col = "blue")

#(e) 
q10est <- mean(q10$q10)
print(q10est)

#(f) A 95% bootstrapped confidence interval
con <- 0.95
low <- (1-con)/2
high <- 1-low

quantile(q10$q10, c(low,high))


```

## 3. Different Population Shapes

```{r}

# A different population shape
x2 <- rexp(500, rate = 1/5)

# (a) histogram of the sample. Adjust the binwidth value so your histogram looks informative.
df <- as.data.frame(x2)
head(df)

ggplot(df, aes(x = x2)) + 
  geom_histogram(fill = "green", col = "blue")

# Calculate the variance of the sample
var(x2)

# (b) Same thing again to verify variance
sampx2 <- sample(x2, size = 500, replace = TRUE)
var(sampx2)  

# (c) Now, we perform bootstrapping using `sample` to sample with replacement
q10 <- c()  

for (num in 1:3000) {  
  x2_sample <- sample(x2, size = 500, replace = TRUE)
  new <- var(x2_sample) 
  q10 <- c(q10, new)  
}

# (d) histogram of approximate sampling distribution of variance
q10 <- as.data.frame(q10)
ggplot(q10, aes(x = q10)) + 
  geom_histogram(fill = "green", col = "blue")

# (e) Calculate the mean of the approximate sampling distribution
q10est <- mean(q10$q10)
print(q10est)

# (f) A 90% bootstrapped confidence interval
con <- 0.90
low <- (1 - con) / 2
high <- 1 - low

# Compute the quantiles for the 90% confidence interval
quantile(q10$q10, c(low, high))
#
```

## 4. Another Population Shapes

1.  <div>

    -   <div>

        ```{r}

        x2 <- c(rnorm(25000, 1, 2), rnorm(25000, 9, 2))

        # (a) histogram of the sample. Adjust the binwidth value so your histogram looks informative.
        df <- as.data.frame(x2)
        head(df)

        ggplot(df, aes(x = x2)) + 
          geom_histogram(fill = "green", col = "blue")


        sd(df$x2)

        # (b) Same thing again to verify standard deviation
        sampx3 <- sample(x2, size = 25000, replace = TRUE)
        sd(sampx3)  


        # (c) Perform bootstrapping using `sample` to sample with replacement
        q10 <- c()  

        for (num in 1:3000) {  # Repeating 3000 times
          x2_sample <- sample(x2, size = 500, replace = TRUE)
          new <- var(x2_sample) 
          q10 <- c(q10, new)  
        }

        # (d) histogram of approximate sampling distribution of variance
        q10 <- as.data.frame(q10)
        ggplot(q10, aes(x = q10)) + 
          geom_histogram(fill = "green", col = "blue")

        # (e) Calculate the mean of the approximate sampling distribution
        q10est <- mean(q10$q10)
        print(q10est)

        # (f) A 99% bootstrapped confidence interval
        con <- 0.99
        low <- (1 - con) / 2
        high <- 1 - low

        # Compute the quantiles for the 99% confidence interval
        quantile(q10$q10, c(low, high))


        ```

        </div>

    </div>
